elements.sent-2411-001: From left to right: the condition inputs followed by results generated using OMG~\cite{kong2024omg (with InstantID~\cite{wang2024instantid ), InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily , and our proposed ID-Patch approach.}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: OMG fails to preserve the hairstyles of the middle person, and creates artifacts for the right woman's hand, possibly because of the inconsistency between its generation stages (explained in Fig.~\ref{fig:omg_fail ).}
elements.sent-2411-004: InstantFamily suffers from ID leakage, resulting the incorrect ID of the middle person.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach preserves the detailed identity of each person.
elements.sent-2411-006: In addition, our approach is 7 times faster than OMG and has less computational overhead than InstantFamily.
elements.sent-2411-007: The ability to synthesize personalized group photos and specify the positions of each identity offers immense creative potential.
elements.sent-2411-008: While such imagery can be visually appealing, it presents significant challenges for existing technologies.
elements.sent-2411-009: A persistent issue is identity (ID) leakage, where injected facial features interfere with one another, resulting in low face resemblance, incorrect positioning, and visual artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-010: Existing methods suffer from limitations such as the reliance on segmentation models, increased runtime, or a high probability of ID leakage.
elements.sent-2411-011: To address these challenges, we propose ID-Patch, a novel method that provides robust association between identities and 2D positions.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our approach generates an ID patch and ID embeddings from the same facial features: the ID patch is positioned on the conditional image for precise spatial control, while the ID embeddings integrate with text embeddings to ensure high resemblance.
elements.sent-2411-013: Experimental results demonstrate that ID-Patch surpasses baseline methods across metrics, such as face ID resemblance, ID-position association accuracy, and generation efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-015: Personalized group photo generation has wide-ranging applications in entertainment, social media, and virtual reality.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, users might want to create a group photo of friends at a virtual party, even if some could not attend in person.
elements.sent-2411-017: This enables the creation of engaging and memorable content that reflects real-life social interactions and relationships.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, this task poses significant challenges, particularly the risk of identity (ID) leakage, where the ID information of one individual inadvertently affects the representation of another at a different position.
elements.sent-2411-019: Such blending compromises the distinctiveness and accuracy of each person’s features.
elements.sent-2411-020: Besides, being able to specify the position of each identity in the image is an important function when users want to create a specific layout of people.
elements.sent-2411-021: Incorrect positioning of identities may lead to undesired results.
elements.sent-2411-022: State-of-the-art group photo personalization methods largely rely on diffusion models \cite{sohl2015deep, song2020denoising, ho2020denoising, dhariwal2021diffusion, balaji2022ediff, nichol2021glide , which deliver superior visual quality when paired with effective prompt engineering~\cite{esser2403scaling, saharia2022photorealistic, chen2023pixart, peebles2023scalable, ma2024sit .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: To address the issue of ID leakage, OMG~\cite{kong2024omg proposes a two-stage workflow: First, it generates an image without ID injection.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Then, it estimates segmentation masks for the individuals in the image and subsequently injects the IDs into each segmented region.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, it depends on a segmentation model, which might fail in challenging cases.
elements.sent-2411-026: Besides, the content generated in the two stages may conflict with each other, leading to reduced visual fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-027: See Fig.\,\ref{fig:teaser and Fig.\,\ref{fig:omg_fail for example.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, OMG requires running the denoising process separately for each identity, resulting in a runtime that increases linearly with the number of people.
elements.sent-2411-029: Alternatively, InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily employs a single-pass generation strategy by manipulating cross-attention masks.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This approach uses approximate head region masks for each ID to direct pixel attention exclusively to specific ID embeddings derived from facial features.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, it struggles to prevent ID leakage due to two key issues: (1) imprecise masks or close proximity of faces, which can cause overlaps, and (2) unintended information propagation through self-attention and convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-032: Please see Fig.\,\ref{fig:teaser and Fig.\,\ref{fig:leak for more details.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In response to these limitations, we propose a novel method named ID-Patch, designed for efficient and robust ID-position association in group photo generation.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our idea is to let the model learn to associate IDs with their spatially designated locations.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, we project facial features onto a small RGB patch called ID patch and token embeddings called ID embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-036: The ID patches are placed onto the conditioning image of a ControlNet~\cite{zhang2023adding according to face locations to ensure precise position control of IDs within the generated images.}
elements.sent-2411-037: ID embeddings are integrated with text embeddings to enhance facial details.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our ID-Patch method seamlessly integrates with various types of spatial conditions, such as poses, canny edges or depth map, enhancing the robustness and flexibility of our method.
elements.sent-2411-039: We summarize our key contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-040: Introduction of ID-Patch, an innovative method for multi-ID image generation that directly links face ID features with their corresponding locations using visual patches in conditioning images, ensuring better resemblance and accurate position control.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our proposed approach enhances the efficiency of multi-ID image generation, by merely adding ID patches to the ControlNet conditioning image and concatenating ID embeddings with text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-042: Removal of the reliance on auxiliary segmentation models, requiring only a single point for ID position control, as opposed to segmented masks or head bounding boxes.
elements.sent-2411-043: Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our proposed ID-Patch over baseline methods in achieving higher fidelity in ID resemblance and higher robustness in position control, particularly in complex images featuring multiple identities.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/teaser/benchmark_0025_563_066_105_omg_s1.png} \caption{OMG first stage} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/teaser/benchmark_0025_563_066_105_seg.png} \caption{Human mask} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/teaser/benchmark_0025_563_066_105_omg.png} \caption{OMG final result} \end{subfigure} \vspace*{-3mm} \caption{Why does OMG fail in Fig.\,\ref{fig:teaser}? OMG relies on a first stage result (no ID awareness) and its human segmentation to control layout. The second stage paints injected IDs within corresponding masks. However, the second pass may conflict with the first stage semantics, leading to deteriorated ID resemblance (long hair in the middle of image (c)) or artifacts (hand in the middle).} \label{fig:omg_fail} \end{center} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/004.jpg} \caption{ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/if.png} \caption{InstantFamily result} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/idp.png} \caption{ID-Patch result} \end{subfigure} \vspace*{-3mm} \caption{ID leakage of InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily}. The text prompt describes a man and a woman, and ID (a) is injected into the left man. However, in (b), InstantFamily suffers from ID leakage problem (right woman is incorrect) even though the cross attention mask (red box) has been manipulated to cover only the man's face region. (c) Our method shows a properly generated result.} \label{fig:leak} \end{center} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.52]{fig/method/pipeline_r3.pdf} \vspace*{-3mm} \caption{Our pipeline. Given a text prompt (e.g. two people shaking hands), $N$ face images and locations, we generate an image with $N$ IDs. We extract face features for each ID and project them into ID patches and ID embeddings. ID patches are rendered on a black canvas (or added on top of a pose image) according to face locations and sent into a ControlNet to control the positions of generated faces. ID embeddings are appended to text embeddings to provide detailed face information to the diffusion model and ControlNet via cross-attention.} \label{fig:pipeline} \vspace*{-3mm}
elements.sent-2411-044: The personalization of diffusion models has emerged as a popular research topic.
elements.sent-2411-045: Fine-tuning or optimization based methods~\cite{gal2022image,wen2024hard,ruiz2023dreambooth,gal2023encoder,ruiz2024hyperdreambooth have the potential to reach high quality results in terms of ID resemblance, but are time-consuming.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Inference-only methods~\cite{wei2023elite,ye2023ip,li2024photomaker,wang2024instantid,he2024imagine,peng2024portraitbooth,zhou2023enhancing,yuan2023inserting eliminate the finetuning process usually by encoding the concept into token embeddings and injecting them into the diffusion models via cross-attention.}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, most of these works focus on single concept injection rather than group photo generation.. Multi-ID Personalization.
elements.sent-2411-048: The main challenge of personalized group photo generation is ID blending, i.e., the identity information of person A may affect the generation of person B at another position.
elements.sent-2411-049: To resolve this issue, OMG \cite{kong2024omg adopts a two-stage solution.}
elements.sent-2411-050: First, a non-customized image is generated and masks of each person are extracted.
elements.sent-2411-051: Second, masked regions are painted using separate customized models and stitched together.
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach is slow and conflicts between stages may cause identity loss.
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, it relies on a segmentation module, which may fail in challenging cases.
elements.sent-2411-054: To avoid running separate customized models, recent works~\cite{xiao2024fastcomposer,wang2024moa,he2024uniportrait let the network to learn to attend to specific concept tokens by supervising the attention mask using segmentation or ID routing.}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, these methods do not allow the specification of the accurate locations of each ID.
elements.sent-2411-056: Although they can be combined with pose conditions, describing the assignment of IDs to positions in text may be hard.
elements.sent-2411-057: InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily provides an opportunity to specify the approximate location of each ID by manipulating the attention masks during both training and inference.}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, no constraints have been imposed to avoid the ID leakage through self-attention and convolutional layers, leading to ID blending, omitting or swapping.. Multiple General Concepts.
elements.sent-2411-059: Besides human generation, there are series of work focusing on the customization of multiple general concepts~\cite{kumari2023multi,avrahami2023break,tewel2023key,avrahami2023spatext,ding2024freecustom,gu2024mix,han2023svdiff,liu2023cones,xiao2024fastcomposer and grounded image generation~\cite{li2023gligen,avrahami2023spatext,Wang_2024_CVPR associating objects with positions.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: GLIGEN~\cite{li2023gligen integrates grounding tokens, which align textual prompts with specific image regions.}
elements.sent-2411-061: SpaText~\cite{avrahami2023spatext and InstanceDiffusion~\cite{Wang_2024_CVPR allows users to define the placement and attributes of objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, these concepts are usually semantically distinct while it is hard to describe the human identities in text and subtle details matter.
elements.sent-2411-063: In this section, we first outline some preliminaries about text-to-image diffusion models and ControlNets.
elements.sent-2411-064: Subsequently, we detail the problem formulation for multi-ID image generation and the design of our modules, including the ID patch and ID embeddings, as well as the training and inference schemes.
elements.sent-2411-066: LDMs \cite{rombach2022high are image generation models that can incorporate conditioning signals into the generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Specifically, during training, an encoder $\mathcal{E}$ of variational autoencoder (VAE) \cite{kingma2013auto encodes an image into a latent space, and a neural network $\epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)$ performs the denoising process within this latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-068: It attempts to predict the original Gaussian noise $\epsilon$ from a noisy latent variable $z_t$, where $t$ represents the diffusion timestep, and $c$ is the conditioning signal, injected via cross-attention.
elements.sent-2411-069: The loss function for an LDM is defined as: \begin{equation}L_\mathrm{LDM} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E},\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1),t}\left[\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)\rVert_2^2\right]\end{equation} ControlNet.
elements.sent-2411-070: The incorporation of ControlNet \cite{zhang2023adding into diffusion models marks a significant advancement in controlled image synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-071: ControlNet is designed to steer the image generation process by applying spatial conditioning signals such as rasterized pose images, depth maps, or canny edges.
elements.sent-2411-072: It introduces a control network that processes these signals and adds their features to the base model's intermediate results.
elements.sent-2411-073: The control network is a trainable copy of the base model's encoder layers and is initialized with zero convolution.
elements.sent-2411-074: This effective integration achieves high quality controlled generation.
elements.sent-2411-075: This paper focuses on efficiently and effectively addressing the problem of multi-ID image generation utilizing conditioning signals that include text prompts, identity features, and face locations.
elements.sent-2411-076: The inputs to our model comprise text prompt embeddings $c_t$, facial features $c_f = (f_0, f_1, \dots, f_{N-1 )$ for $N$ distinct identities, and their corresponding facial locations $c_l = (l_0, l_1, \dots, l_{N-1 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The facial location $l_i = (x_i, y_i)$ identifies the xy-coordinates of the nose tip for each identity within the intended image.
elements.sent-2411-078: The goal is to synthesize an image following the text description with each given face located in the designated place.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.23\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/id_token/faces.png} \caption{ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3625\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/id_token/benchmark_0014_011_006_no_id_token.png} \caption{w/o ID embeddings} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.3625\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/id_token/benchmark_0014_011_006.png} \caption{with ID embeddings} \end{subfigure} \caption{Effectiveness of ID embedding. Without ID embeddings, one can distinguish between the two people but the resemblance is low. Incorporating ID embeddings significantly improves face resemblance.} \label{fig:id_token} \end{center} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.sent-2411-079: As discussed in Sec.~\ref{ssec:prelim , ControlNet effectively integrates spatial conditional signals into diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Building on this capability, we introduce a method to encode ID location information using condition images, subsequently processed by ControlNet to guide ID localization within the generated image.
elements.sent-2411-081: Our approach features ID conditions manifesting as both patches and embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, ID patches, precisely aligned with facial locations, are integrated via ControlNet to ensure accurate identity localization, while ID embeddings are utilized via cross-attention mechanisms in the LDM to enhance the detailed representation of facial features, as illustrated in Fig.\,\ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Both ID patches and ID embeddings are derived from facial features, enabling a synergistic association between them, with both projection processes facilitated by the Perceiver Resampler \cite{ye2023ip .}
elements.sent-2411-084: For ID patches, we convert the facial feature $f_i$ into an identity-specific patch $p_i \in \mathbb{R ^{P \times P \times 3}$, functioning similarly to a QR code, uniquely identifying each person and easily distinguishable from others.}
elements.sent-2411-085: These ID patches are placed on a black canvas at specified facial locations to create an image $I$, which then serves as the conditioning input for ControlNet, thereby providing precise identity localization information.
elements.sent-2411-086: As depicted in Fig.\,\ref{fig:id_token , while ID patches alone are generally sufficient for distinguishing between identities, they may not always achieve optimal results in generating images with high ID resemblance.}
elements.sent-2411-087: To address this limitation, and inspired by InstantFamily \cite{kim2024instantfamily , we also incorporate ID embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-088: These embeddings are designed to inject detailed facial appearance information into the diffusion model, thus enhancing the resemblance of the generated identities to their real-life counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, ID embeddings $w_i \in \mathbb{R ^{2048 \times M}$, share the same token dimension 2048 as the SDXL text embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-090: As shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:pipeline , these embeddings are appended to text embeddings $c_t$ to form an extended text embedding $c_t' = [c_t, w_0, w_1, \dots, w_{N-1 ]$, which is then fed into both the UNet and ControlNet through cross-attention mechanisms.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Since human pose conditions are often utilized to enhance group photo generation, we can combine our ID-Patch approach with the pose conditions for multi-id image generation.
elements.sent-2411-092: As illustrated in Fig.\,\ref{fig:idpatch_on_pose , the ID patches can be overlaid onto a pose-conditioned image, creating a composite condition image that encodes both identity positioning and pose information.}
elements.sent-2411-093: It is important to mention that while our method can incorporate pose information, it does not inherently require it; pose is just an optional enhancement, one can also choose other conditions like canny edge, depth map for specific applications.
elements.sent-2411-094: The adaptability of our ID-Patch approach presents enhanced control over generated results yet without incurring the extra overhead of an additional ControlNet branch.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.1\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/idpatch_on_pose/faces.png} \caption{ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.29\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/idpatch_on_pose/benchmark_0020_003_019_009_nopose.png} \caption{Result w/o pose} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.29\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/idpatch_on_pose/pose.png} \caption{ID patch on pose} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.29\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/idpatch_on_pose/benchmark_0020_003_019_009.png} \caption{Result with pose} \end{subfigure} \caption{ID-Patch combined with pose conditions. Provided with user ID images in (a), our method can generate results with only the specifications of nose tip locations as shown in (b). Merging ID patches with pose images (c) enhances spatial control over the generated results as seen in (d), without incurring any computational overhead.} \label{fig:idpatch_on_pose} \end{center} \vspace*{-6mm}
elements.sent-2411-095: The training of our model employs a conventional diffusion loss function, defined as follows: \begin{equation}L = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E},\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1),t}[\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon\theta(z_t, t, I, c'_t)\rVert_2^2]\end{equation} Initially, one might consider training the models end-to-end from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-096: However, our experiments reveal that such a method does not consistently ensure accurate ID localization, as depicted in Fig.\,\ref{fig:single_stage .}
elements.sent-2411-097: This shortcoming likely stems from the network’s tendency to preferentially inject ID information into ID embeddings, rather than ID patches, resulting in non-distinctive ID patches that could potentially confuse ControlNet.
elements.sent-2411-098: In the single-stage model, as shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:single_stage , the ID patches exhibit only subtle differences between different individuals.}
elements.sent-2411-099: To resolve this, we introduce a two-stage training approach.
elements.sent-2411-100: In the initial stage, ID embeddings are omitted, compelling the network to prioritize the incorporation of ID information into ID patches, thereby enhancing their distinctiveness.
elements.sent-2411-101: In the subsequent stage, ID embeddings are introduced and the model is fine-tuned to further improve the resemblance of the generated images.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/single_stage/pose_refined.pdf} \caption{Pose + ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/single_stage/single_stage.pdf} \caption{Single-stage train} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/single_stage/two_stage.pdf} \caption{Two-stage train} \end{subfigure} \caption{Two-stage training to improve positioning robustness. Given pose and ID conditions in (a), single-stage training cannot fully prevent the incorrect face positioning issue. For example, in (b), the man is mistakenly placed at the central bottom position, generating an inharmonious result. (c) Two-stage training is introduced to solve this problem. As can be seen on the top row of this figure, the ID patches from our two-stage training approach are visually more distinguishable from each other, compared to the ones from single-stage training. Our experimental results prove that this is critical in solving the ID leak issues.} \label{fig:single_stage} \end{center} \vspace*{-8mm}
elements.sent-2411-102: We also implement a two-stage inference process that further enhances performance.
elements.sent-2411-103: Specifically, during the first $\sim$20\% of the timesteps, only ID patches are utilized.
elements.sent-2411-104: This focuses the early denoising steps on establishing basic ID localization without the detailed facial features encoded in ID embeddings, which are not as effective in the initial phases.
elements.sent-2411-105: For the remaining timesteps, both ID patches and ID embeddings are employed, allowing the finer facial details to be incorporated as the image clarity improves.
elements.sent-2411-106: This strategy not only ensures better ID localization but also minimizes interference with text conditions, thus enhancing the model’s ability to follow text prompts.
elements.sent-2411-107: Facial features $f_i$ we use are 512-dimensional vectors derived from ArcFace~\cite{deng2019arcface .}
elements.sent-2411-108: During training, we utilize the SDXL diffusion model \cite{podellsdxl .}
elements.sent-2411-109: During the inference phase, we employ the further finetuned SDXL, namely Juggernaut-X-v10$^1$ \footnote{$^1$ https://huggingface.co/RunDiffusion/Juggernaut-X-v10 , as base model to enhance portrait quality.}
elements.sent-2411-111: As mentioned in Sec.~\ref{ssec:twostage , we divide these timesteps into two phases to regulate the influence of identity features: the initial 20\% of timesteps operate without ID token embeddings to focus on the general style, followed by the remaining timesteps which incorporate ID token embeddings to refine the identity-specific aspects of the images.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The default size of ID-patch is $P\times P = 64 \times 64$ and the default length of ID embeddings is $M = 16$.. Dataset.
elements.sent-2411-113: Our training set consists of 17 million single-person and 1.95 million multi-person images.
elements.sent-2411-114: These images are obtained from purchased data and publicly available sources.
elements.sent-2411-115: For evaluation, we utilize two specialized datasets: 1) Style Dataset comprises 40 styles with pose and text prompt definitions, ranging from one to eight faces.
elements.sent-2411-116: Each style is paired with 20 randomly sampled unique identity combinations for quantitative evaluation.. Baselines.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our analysis includes two state-of-the-art multi-ID generation methods as baselines: 1) OMG~\cite{kong2024omg : This method incorporates multiple identity features into separate segmented regions through latent stitching.}
elements.sent-2411-118: It can be paired with a single ID injection method InstantID~\cite{wang2024instantid for multi-ID generation.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Performance comparison across four dimensions: identity resemblance, ID-position association accuracy, text alignment, and generation time. For qualitative metrics to evaluate similarity or accuracy, higher is better. Our approach significantly outperforms baseline methods in terms of resemblance, association, and generation time, with similar text alignment scores.} \vspace*{-3mm} 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{c|cccc} [1pt] Methods ID $\uparrow$ Association $\uparrow$ Text $\uparrow$ Time (s) $\downarrow$ OMG + InstantID 0.644 0.926 0.271 84.83 InstantFamily 0.683 0.862 0.278 10.02 ID-Patch (Ours) 0.751 0.958 0.273 9.69 [1pt] \end{tabular} } \vspace*{-2mm} \label{tab:compare}
elements.figure-2411-008: \vspace*{-4mm} \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.28\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/compare_n_face/id_compare_n_face_r2.pdf} \caption{Identity Resemblance} \end{subfigure} \quad\quad \begin{subfigure}{0.28\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/compare_n_face/loc_compare_n_face_r2.pdf} \caption{Association Accuracy} \end{subfigure} \quad\quad \begin{subfigure}{0.28\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/compare_n_face/time_compare_n_face.pdf} \caption{Generation Time} \end{subfigure} \caption{Performance evaluation of different model generations from three different aspects: identity resemblance, location accuracy, and generation time. As face number increases, the ID and location metric scores of OMG and Instantfamily drop much more significantly than our approach. Our approach also achieves near-constant generation time, in contrast to OMG's linearly increasing running time. } \label{fig:compare_n_face_all} \end{center} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.sent-2411-119: We evaluate the methods in a pose-conditioned setting, because pose is a requirement of InstantFamily, and without pose condition, OMG is not able to generate faces at specified locations for its first stage.
elements.sent-2411-120: Besides, pose condition makes the head size consistent, which is easier for resemblance evaluation and reduces the impact of head sizes on generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-121: For comprehensive evaluation, we consider four critical dimensions: 1) Identity resemblance: we measure the identity resemblance by calculating the cosine similarity (CosSim) between the facial features of faces in the generated images and the reference faces.
elements.sent-2411-122: For fairness, we use FaceNet \cite{schroff2015facenet , a face model different from the ArcFace model used for training.}
elements.sent-2411-123: In other words, we calculate the similarity between the $i$-th generated face and the $j$-th input face.
elements.sent-2411-124: Let $s(i) = \arg \max_{0 \leq j \leq N-1 \text{CosSim (\Tilde{f _{i ^{\text{\text{gen , \Tilde{f _j) $ be the face index among all input faces that is most similar to the $i$-th generated face, where $\Tilde{f _{i ^{\text{gen }$, $\Tilde{f _j$ denote the FaceNet features of the $i$-th generated face and the $j$-th input face respectively, and $N$ is the number of faces in this image.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: When $s(i)=i$, the $i$-th generated face has the correct association.
elements.sent-2411-126: The identity-position association accuracy is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{location_distance} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \mathbf{1}{\left\{ i = s(i) \right\}}\end{equation} This measurement assesses the correct placement of each identity within the image.
elements.sent-2411-127: Cosine similarity is used to compare textual features extracted by the CLIP text encoder with visual features extracted by the CLIP image encoder from the generated images~\cite{radford2021learning .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c} [1pt] \multirow{1}[4]{*}[0.8cm]{ \rotatebox{90}{ {ID + ID Location} } } \begin{minipage}{0.14\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0018_474_078_id_refined_text.pdf}\end{minipage} \vspace*{1mm} \begin{minipage}{0.18\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0027_070_036_462_id_refined_text.pdf}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.205\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0045_277_478_269_089_132_id_refined_text.pdf}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.18\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0071_431_265_295_337_105_301_556_589_id_refined_text.pdf}\end{minipage} \multirow{1}[4]{*}[0.8cm]{ \rotatebox{90}{ {Generation Result} } } \begin{minipage}{0.14\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0018_474_078.png}\end{minipage} \vspace*{1mm} \begin{minipage}{0.18\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0027_070_036_462.png}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.205\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0045_277_478_269_089_132.png}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.18\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/nopose/benchmark_0071_431_265_295_337_105_301_556_589_seed7484.png}\end{minipage} [1pt] \end{tabular} } \caption{ID-Patch pose-free generation. Here the condition image is only ID patches rendered onto a black canvas without any pose condition. The generation is conditioned on individual face locations while corresponding head sizes and body poses are inferred implicitly. Our method can accommodate a large number of people with diverse ethnic backgrounds, yet generating visually appealing group photo results. See ``Evaluation Metrics" for reasons why we compare with baseline approaches only in pose-conditioned settings. } \label{fig:nopose} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.sent-2411-128: As listed in Tab.\,\ref{tab:compare , ID-Patch excels in identity and association metrics with scores of 0.751 and 0.958, respectively, showcasing its robust capability to maintain identity consistency and achieve precise placement in generated images.}
elements.sent-2411-129: There is no significant difference in text alignment scores between compared methods.
elements.sent-2411-130: Notably, ID-Patch achieves the fastest image generation time.
elements.sent-2411-131: Further details on the performance with varying face numbers are shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:compare_n_face_all .}
elements.sent-2411-132: As we observed no deterioration in text alignment with an increase in the number of faces across all methods, we have relegated the corresponding figure to the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-133: As the number of faces increases, both OMG and InstantFamily experience significant performance drops due to ID leakage.
elements.sent-2411-134: Although ID-Patch also shows a decline, it is less severe, mainly because the increase in face numbers reduces the average face area, adversely affecting the quality of smaller faces produced by the SDXL models.
elements.sent-2411-135: Moreover, the presence of more faces slightly extends the image generation time due to the increased demand for facial feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-136: InstantFamily is slightly slower than our approach due to the overhead of masked attention.
elements.sent-2411-137: For OMG, injecting identity features into designated segmented areas causes a substantial increase in generation time; it takes about 2 minutes to generate an image with eight people, whereas ID-Patch requires only about 10 seconds.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Ablation study results for our proposed ID-Patch variants, designed to assess the impact of different modules (patch projection and token projection) and training/inferencing schemes on the performance of multi-ID image generation. The full method achieves the best ID resemblance and ID-position association, indicating the necessity of each module.} \vspace*{-3mm} 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{c|ccc} [1pt] Variants ID $\uparrow$ Association $\uparrow$ Text $\uparrow$ No patch projection 0.368 0.345 0.271 No token projection 0.565 0.895 0.284 Single stage training 0.660 0.784 0.271 Single stage inference 0.750 0.957 0.270 FULL 0.751 0.958 0.273 [1pt] \end{tabular} } \label{tab:ablation}
elements.sent-2411-139: We conduct an ablation study on variants of the proposed method in Tab.\,\ref{tab:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-140: The performance notably degrades when patch projection is omitted, leading to poor location alignment as the algorithm attempts to infer ID locations based on indirect cues such as gender and pose.
elements.sent-2411-141: Similarly, omitting token projection reduces resemblance by eliminating essential facial details, quantitatively validates the finding of Fig.\,\ref{fig:id_token .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Single-stage training results in indistinguishable ID patches (as shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:single_stage) , which impairs effective positioning.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Our proposed ID-Patch, which utilizes two-stage inference, significantly enhances both text alignment and ID positioning over its single-stage counterpart, although it does not alter ID resemblance.
elements.sent-2411-144: This may be because ID embeddings contribute minimally to facial detail representation in the initial denoising steps but are more influential in subsequent text following and ID positioning.
elements.sent-2411-145: Our ID-Patch model can be operated effectively without the need for pose conditioning, a flexibility that permits its integration with other conditions for image generation.
elements.sent-2411-146: This capability enables the use of ID-Patch in versatile configurations, whether plug-and-play or co-trained, which we will explore in subsequent subsections.
elements.sent-2411-147: In the pose-free mode, the ControlNet uses a conditioning image that solely features ID patches against a black background.
elements.sent-2411-148: The placement of each ID patch at the tip of the nose ensures accurate preservation of facial locations during image synthesis, while head sizes and poses are variably generated based on the initial noise.
elements.sent-2411-149: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:nopose , this methodology allows our model to produce aesthetically pleasing group photographs of different number of individuals across a variety of settings, demonstrating the robustness and adaptability of our approach.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.2\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/plug_n_play/stitched_98_323.png} \caption{ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.39\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/plug_n_play/canny_condition_2.jpg} \caption{Canny edge condition} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.39\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/plug_n_play/result_canny_pnp_8_4.jpg} \caption{ID-Patch result} \end{subfigure} \caption{Combination of pose-free ID-Patch ControlNet and pretrained Canny edge ControlNet. We can generate background and clothing details, with high quality ID injection.} \label{fig:plug_and_play} \end{center} \vspace*{-7mm}
elements.sent-2411-150: The pose-free version of ID-Patch is designed to be compatible with other ControlNets (plug-and-play).
elements.sent-2411-151: As illustrated in Fig.\,\ref{fig:plug_and_play , it is possible to combine our ID-Patch ControlNet the Canny edge ControlNet to achieve more precise control over the final image outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-152: As illustrated in Fig.\,\ref{fig:fail , our method encounters two primary limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-153: First, our implementation is based on the SDXL model, which occasionally produces inaccuracies in body and finger anatomy.
elements.sent-2411-154: Transitioning to a more advanced base model could potentially rectify this issue.
elements.sent-2411-155: Second, facial features in the current model may incorporate lighting and expression details that are not fully separated from identity information, potentially compromising the generation quality.
elements.sent-2411-156: Enriching our training dataset with multiple images of the same identities, showcasing varied expressions and lighting conditions, might help alleviate this problem.
elements.sent-2411-157: Although the resemblance has been improved with our approach, there still exists a performance gap between generating a single person and multiple people (Fig.\,\ref{fig:compare_n_face_all (a) ).}
elements.sent-2411-158: This means generating personalized group photo remains a challenging problem and requires further study.
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.42\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fail/benchmark_0065_223_588_150_545_036_370_166_fail.png} \caption{Abnormal anatomy} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.56\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/fail/limitation_makeup.pdf} \caption{Over-fitting input} \end{subfigure} \caption{Limitations. (a) Abnormal anatomy can be found in complex scenes, e.g. the forearms in the red box. (b) When input faces contain strong shadows, our method struggles to fully disentangle user's ID in generated result.} \label{fig:fail} \end{center} \vspace*{-8mm}
elements.sent-2411-159: In conclusion, our novel method, ID-Patch, markedly enhances identity resemblance and positioning in group photo generation.
elements.sent-2411-160: By embedding each identity feature within a distinct patch and utilizing ControlNet to accurately place each identity at the designated spatial locations, we successfully reduce ID leakage.
elements.sent-2411-161: Our approach seamlessly integrates with additional conditioning signals such as pose control.
elements.sent-2411-162: Experimental results underscore the superiority of ID-Patch, particularly in complex scenarios involving more than three identities.
elements.sent-2411-163: This work paves the way for future explorations in multi-ID image generation.
elements.sent-2411-164: Potential future research directions include leveraging multiple images of the same individual from different angles to further enhance identity resemblance and simultaneously controlling location and facial expressions using the patch technique.
elements.sent-2411-165: Societal Impact: Our work studies ID injection from a technical perspective and is not intended for malicious use.
elements.sent-2411-166: However, inappropriate usage might generate undesired fake images.
elements.sent-2411-167: Users should be aware of the ethical implications and use it responsibly.
elements.sent-2411-168: Our training set comprises a robust collection of images, totaling 17 million single-person and 1.95 million multi-person instances.
elements.sent-2411-169: These images are sourced from both purchased data and publicly available sources.
elements.sent-2411-170: Each image has been carefully cropped and resized according to predefined specifications and subsequently organized into distinct data buckets based on shape categories.
elements.sent-2411-171: BLIP-2~\cite{li2023blip is employed to obtain image captions.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Face localization in images is performed using the MTCNN framework \cite{zhang2016joint .}
elements.sent-2411-173: For generating the pose conditions, we employ the HRNet-DEKR model \cite{geng2021bottom for pose estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-174: To enhance the accuracy of this estimation, we compute the average distance between facial keypoints detected by MTCNN (considered as ground truth) and those estimated by HRNet-DEKR.
elements.sent-2411-175: Poses with a large keypoint distance are filtered out to ensure precision.
elements.sent-2411-176: The network is trained using 8 NVIDIA H100 GPUs with batch size=4, learning rate=1e-05, weight decay = 0.01, using AdaFactor optimizer~\cite{shazeer2018adafactor .}
elements.sent-2411-177: We train it for 720k iterations for stage 1, and stage 2 usually converges around 1 million iterations.
elements.sent-2411-178: We select the best model based on a 20 people validation set.
elements.sent-2411-179: Because there is no official publicly available code for InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily , for fairness, we implemented it based on the SDXL base model~\cite{podellsdxl , and trained it using the same training data as our model.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Details regarding the text alignment scores with varying numbers of faces are illustrated in Fig.\,\ref{fig:text_n_face_all .}
elements.sent-2411-181: It is evident that all three methods exhibit comparable performance in terms of text alignment.
elements.sent-2411-182: Beside, we do not observe a significant change of the text alignment score across different number of faces.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/compare_n_face/text_compare_n_face.pdf} \caption{Performance evaluation of different model generations from the perspective of the text alignment score. There is no significant trend or difference between different number of faces. All methods achieve similar scores.} \label{fig:text_n_face_all}
elements.sent-2411-183: Additional pose-free generated images of our proposed ID-Patch can be found in Fig.\,\ref{fig:nopose_supp , where the generation is conditioned on individual face locations while corresponding head sizes and body poses are inferred implicitly.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Our method can accommodate a large number of people with diverse ethnic backgrounds, yet generating visually appealing group photo results.
elements.sent-2411-185: Additional comparisons of pose-conditioned generated images are available in Fig.\,\ref{fig:add_compare_1 and Fig.\,\ref{fig:add_compare_2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-186: In these visualizations, our proposed method, ID-Patch, consistently achieves robust ID association without ID leakage.
elements.sent-2411-187: In contrast, the other two methods experience significant ID leakage as the total number of faces in the generated images increases.

elements.sent-2411-188: Personalized group photo generation has wide-ranging applications in entertainment, advertising, social media, and virtual reality.
elements.sent-2411-189: This technology enables the creation of engaging and memorable content that reflects real-life social interactions and relationships.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, this task poses significant challenges, particularly the risk of identity (ID) leakage, where the identity information of one individual inadvertently affects the representation of another at a different position.
elements.sent-2411-191: State-of-the-art group photo personalization methods largely rely on diffusion models \cite{sohl2015deep, song2020denoising, ho2020denoising, dhariwal2021diffusion, balaji2022ediff, nichol2021glide,zhang2024defensive, wang2024edit, zhang2024unlearncanvas, sui2024disdet,xiao2024coap,pmlr-v202-xiao23e , which deliver superior visual quality when paired with effective prompt engineering~\cite{esser2403scaling, saharia2022photorealistic, chen2023pixart, peebles2023scalable, ma2024sit,zhang2024generate .}}
elements.sent-2411-192: To address the issue of ID leakage, OMG~\cite{kong2024omg first generates an image without ID injection.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Our idea is to let the model learn to associate input IDs with their spatially designated locations.
elements.sent-2411-194: Specifically, we project facial features onto a small RGB image patch called ID patch and token embeddings called ID embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-195: ID-Patch Method: We introduce a novel approach for multi-ID image generation that directly links face ID features with their locations using visual patches, ensuring accurate resemblance and position control.
elements.sent-2411-196: Efficiency Improvement: Our method enhances the efficiency of multi-ID image generation by simplifying the process to adding ID patches and concatenating ID embeddings, reducing computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-197: Simplified Control: We eliminate the need for auxiliary segmentation models, requiring only a single point for ID position control, thereby increasing robustness and reducing complexity.
elements.sent-2411-198: Superior Performance: Our approach achieves higher fidelity in ID resemblance and better position control, especially in complex images with multiple identities, as demonstrated by our experimental results.
elements.figure-2411-017: \begin{center} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/004.jpg} \caption{ID} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/if.png} \caption{InstantFamily result} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{fig/leak/idp.png} \caption{ID-Patch result} \end{subfigure} \vspace*{-3mm} \caption{ID leakage of InstantFamily~\cite{kim2024instantfamily}. The text prompt describes a man and a woman standing together, and ID (a) is only injected into the left man. } \label{fig:leak} \end{center} \vspace*{-5mm}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[scale=0.52]{fig/method/pipeline_r3.pdf} \vspace*{-3mm} \caption{Our pipeline. Given a text prompt (e.g., two people shaking hands), $N$ face images and locations, we generate an image with $N$ IDs. We extract face features for each ID and project them into ID patches and ID embeddings. ID patches are rendered on a black canvas (or added on top of a pose image) according to face locations and sent into a ControlNet to control the positions of generated faces. ID embeddings are appended to text embeddings to provide detailed face information to the diffusion model and ControlNet via cross-attention.} \label{fig:pipeline} \vspace*{-3mm}
elements.sent-2411-199: To resolve this issue, OMG \cite{kong2024omg performs separate denoising processes.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Specifically, during training, an encoder $\mathcal{E}$ of variational autoencoder (VAE) \cite{kingma2013auto encodes an image $x$ into a latent space, and a neural network $\epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)$ performs the denoising process within this latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-201: The loss function for an LDM is defined as: \begin{equation}L_\mathrm{LDM} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E}(x),\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1),t}\left[\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, c)\rVert_2^2\right]\end{equation} ControlNet.
elements.sent-2411-202: The training of our model employs a conventional diffusion loss function, defined as follows: \begin{equation}L = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{E}(x),\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1),t}[\lVert\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(z_t, t, I, c'_t)\rVert_2^2]\end{equation} Initially, one might consider training the models end-to-end from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-203: During the inference phase, we employ the further finetuned SDXL, namely Juggernaut-X-v10\footnote{https://huggingface.co/RunDiffusion/Juggernaut-X-v10 , as base model to enhance portrait quality.}
elements.sent-2411-204: We set the ID token injection ratio to 0.8 to regulate the influence of identity features, where the initial 20\% of timesteps operate without ID token embeddings to focus on the general style, followed by the remaining 80\% timesteps which incorporate ID token embeddings to refine the identity-specific aspects of the images.
elements.sent-2411-205: The default size of ID-patch is $P\times P = 64 \times 64$ and the default length of ID embeddings is $M = 16$.
elements.sent-2411-206: We select the best model based on a 20 people validation set.. Dataset.

The full method achieves the best ID resemblance and ID-position association, indicating the necessity of each module.} \vspace*{-3mm} 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{c|ccc} [1pt] Variants ID $\uparrow$ Association $\uparrow$ Text $\uparrow$ No patch projection 0.368 0.345 0.271 No token projection 0.565 0.895 0.284 Single stage training 0.660 0.784 0.271 ID token injection ratio = 1.0 0.750 0.957 0.270 FULL 0.751 0.958 0.273 [1pt] \end{tabular} } \label{tab:ablation}
elements.sent-2411-207: The default setting with an ID token injection ratio of 0.8 improves text alignment compared to injecting the ID token at every timestep, while maintaining the same level of identity resemblance.
elements.sent-2411-208: This improvement may arise because ID embeddings have little effect on facial detail during the early denoising steps, but become more influential in the later stages.
elements.sent-2411-209: It is important to note that even without the inference trick (i.e., using the ID token injection ratio), our proposed method still outperforms all baseline approaches.
